content.text: For the languages which are just simple configurations, like in the following pull request, wouldn't it be more logical to have a way to set up any language segment just using configuration in Oh My Posh and not pollute our segment landscape? We just resolve any possible executable with a version using a generic language segment that exposes the properties set in this PR? https://github.com/JanDeDobbeleer/oh-my-posh/pull/7691

content.text:
Looking at [language.go](src/segments/language.go) and [fortran.go](src/segments/fortran.go), you're largely right — the `Language` base already *is* that generic engine (executable + args + regex + extensions/project-files context), and something like GCC is genuinely just ~25 lines of config bolted onto it. A generic `language`/`executable` segment type that takes `tooling`, `extensions`, `project_files` etc. directly in the user's config would eliminate a lot of this boilerplate (new `.go` file + test + schema entry + mdx page) for the "one executable, one version regex" case.

The tradeoff: it works cleanly for simple cases, but several existing language segments aren't actually simple — they need custom `loadContext`/multi-tool fallback (asdf, fvm), version-file matching, or bespoke template fields (Node's `.PackageManagerIcon` (src/segments/node.go), Go's toolchain detection). You'd end up with a generic segment for the easy 70% and dedicated Go segments for the rest anyway, plus you lose per-language schema autocomplete/validation and a dedicated docs page unless you invest in generic schema tooling for it. I'd frame it as "add a generic executable-version segment for the trivial cases" rather than "replace the segment-per-language pattern" — worth raising as an issue/discussion on the repo before committing to it as a direction.
You are absolutely right. What would we then need to change, and which segments can we replace with a configuration instead? What would a good name be for that segment?

# Code Changes

The workflow for going from an issue, pull request, idea, or feature request to shipped code.
Follow the phases in order. Analysis always comes first; code comes last.

Each phase has a reference file with the full instructions. **Read the reference file when you
enter the phase** — not before, and never skip it because the phase "looks obvious".

## Roles

Assign work based on model capability:

| Role         | Capability tier                               | Owns                                      |
| ------------ | --------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------- |
| Orchestrator | Strongest reasoning model available           | Analysis, plan, supervision, verification |
| Implementer  | Mid-tier coder; small model for trivial edits | Executing one pinned, self-contained task |

Concrete model names per vendor (Anthropic, OpenAI, Google) and how to run the split in Claude
Code, GitHub Copilot, Codex, or IDE agents: [references/model-tiers.md](references/model-tiers.md).

When the current agent already runs on an implementer-tier model, skip delegation: plan and
execute directly, still following every phase. Never delegate to a model more capable than the
current one.

## The flow

1. **Analyze** ([references/analyze.md](references/analyze.md)) — root cause and scope,
   validated against the code, never against the report alone.
2. **Plan** ([references/plan.md](references/plan.md)) — pinned spec, task split, parallel vs
   sequential, workspace per task.
3. **Delegate** ([references/delegate.md](references/delegate.md)) — match each task to the
   cheapest capable executor.
4. **Supervise** ([references/supervise.md](references/supervise.md)) — monitor, unblock, and
   critically review implementer output.
5. **Verify** ([references/verify.md](references/verify.md)) — quality gates plus functional
   proof, never delegated.
6. **Deliver** ([references/deliver.md](references/deliver.md)) — conventional commits and an
   outcome-first report.

**Stop gate:** Phase 1 ends with reporting the analysis and proposed approach to the user and
waiting for a go. Skip the gate only when the user already gave the go in the request itself
("do it", "fix it and commit", "implement with Sonnet").

## Special cases

These entry points replace or extend Phase 1; the rest of the flow applies unchanged.

- Pull request review comments →
  [references/pr-review-comments.md](references/pr-review-comments.md)
- Issue triage → [references/issue-triage.md](references/issue-triage.md)

content.text:
## Analysis

**Why this is tractable:** [`Language`](src/segments/language.go) (the base struct every language segment embeds) is already a generic "run an executable, regex out a version, decide visibility" engine. `extensions`, `folders`, `display_mode`, `home_enabled`, `missing_command_text`, `version_url_template`, `cache_duration`, and even *which* of a segment's predefined tools to use ([`tooling` option](src/segments/language.go:137-138), consumed by `loadTooling()` at [language.go:215-229](src/segments/language.go:215)) are **already config-overridable**. The one thing that isn't configurable is the `tooling` *map itself* — the `{executable, args, regex, version_url_template}` tuples — which today is hardcoded Go per segment.

**Survey of `src/segments/*.go`** — of the ~26 language segments, these are pure config (extensions/project files + a `tooling` map + `defaultTooling`, nothing else):

Fortran, Clojure, Crystal, Julia, Kotlin, Nim, Lua, OCaml, Perl, Php, Swift, V, Vala, Zig, Rust, Elixir, R, Ruby, Dart — 19 segments, plus GCC from the PR that prompted this. All of them could be deleted as `.go` files and expressed entirely as config against a single generic implementation.

These need to stay bespoke — they branch on option values, read files, or read env vars beyond the tool-selection pattern:
- [java.go](src/segments/java.go) — builds a dynamic executable path from `JAVA_HOME`
- [haskell.go](src/segments/haskell.go) — `stack_ghc_mode` option drives conditional tool switching + parent-file check
- [mojo.go](src/segments/mojo.go) — Pixi venv detection (`PIXI_ENVIRONMENT_NAME`)
- [golang.go](src/segments/golang.go) — parses `go.mod`/`go.work` for the version instead of running a binary
- [python.go](src/segments/python.go) (225 lines) / [node.go](src/segments/node.go) (158 lines) / [dotnet.go](src/segments/dotnet.go) — venv/package-manager/SDK-file logic

## Proposed change

1. **New segment type**, e.g. `type Language struct { Language }`-style wrapper (needs a non-abstract name — see below) registered in [segment_types.go](src/config/segment_types.go), implementing `Template()` → `languageTemplate`.
2. **One new option, `tools`**: an ordered array of `{name, executable, args, regex, version_url_template}` objects, consumed in `Enabled()` to populate `l.tooling` and `l.defaultTooling` — the only piece the base engine can't already do from config.
3. **One small gap to close**: `project_files` isn't exposed as an option today (only `extensions`/`folders` are) — needed since some candidates key off files like `shard.yml` or `Cargo.toml` rather than extensions.
4. **`getName()` can't be reused as-is** — it derives the segment's cache key by inspecting the calling `.go` filename via `runtime.Caller` ([language.go:141-145](src/segments/language.go:141)), which breaks when there's no per-language file. The generic segment needs an explicit `name` field in config to key its version cache.
5. **schema.json + one new docs page** for the generic segment, plus updates to `segment_types.go`.

## Name

`language` — it's free (not used as a `SegmentType` string today), matches the existing docs grouping (`/docs/segments/languages/`), and reads naturally: `"type": "language"` with `"name": "gcc"`. `executable` was the alternative but undersells the extension/project-file-triggered visibility logic that's specific to this family of segments, not to arbitrary executables.

## Out of scope

Non-`Language`-based tool segments (bazel, buf, cmake, gitversion, mvn, helm, etc.) use a different pattern and aren't part of this. Python/Node/Go/Java/Haskell/Mojo/Dotnet stay bespoke.

## Open question

Two ways to land this, different blast radius:
- **A — additive only:** ship `language` for new/custom languages; leave the 19 existing simple segments' `.go` files untouched. Low risk, doesn't reduce existing maintenance surface.
- **B — migrate too:** keep the existing `type: "fortran"` etc. strings and docs pages working (no user-facing config break), but reimplement their registration to instantiate the generic engine from a small built-in data table instead of 19 separate structs — collapsing ~500 lines of near-duplicate Go into one table.

I'd lean **B** since your original complaint was PR/maintenance burden, and it's backward compatible — but it's a bigger, higher-verification-cost change than A. Which do you want?
Create a plan for B and review with Fable 5

99	// Template resolves the option value as a template and returns the resolved string.
100	// This allows using template syntax like {{ .Env.MY_API_KEY }} in configuration values.
101	// If template rendering fails, it returns the original string value.
102	func (m Map) Template(option Option, defaultValue string, context any) string {
103		value := m.String(option, defaultValue)
104		if value == "" {
105			return value
106		}
107	
108		resolved, err := template.Render(value, context)
109		if err != nil {
110			debugf("%s: template error, using raw value: %s", option, err)
111			return value
112		}
113	
114		debugf("%s (template resolved): %s", option, resolved)
115		return resolved
116	}
117	
118	func (m Map) Color(option Option, defaultValue color.Ansi) color.Ansi {
119		val, found := m[option]
120		if !found {
121			debugf("%s: %s", option, defaultValue)
122			return defaultValue
123		}
124	
125		colorString := color.Ansi(fmt.Sprint(val))
126		if color.IsAnsiColorName(colorString) {
127			debugf("%s: %s", option, colorString)
128			return colorString
129		}
130	
131		values := regex.FindNamedRegexMatch(`(?P<color>#[A-Fa-f0-9]{6}|[A-Fa-f0-9]{3}|p:.*)`, colorString.String())
132		if values != nil && values["color"] != "" {
133			value := color.Ansi(values["color"])
134			debugf("%s: %s", option, value)
135			return value
136		}
137	
138		debugf("%s: %s", option, defaultValue)
139		return defaultValue
140	}
141	
142	func (m Map) Bool(option Option, defaultValue bool) bool {
143		val, found := m[option]
144		if !found {
145			debugf("%s: %t", option, defaultValue)
146			return defaultValue
147		}
148		boolValue, ok := val.(bool)
149		if !ok {
150			debugf("%s: %t", option, defaultValue)
151			return defaultValue
152		}
153		debugf("%s: %t", option, boolValue)
154		return boolValue
155	}
156	
157	func (m Map) Float64(option Option, defaultValue float64) float64 {
158		val, found := m[option]
159		if !found {
160			debugf("%s: %f", option, defaultValue)
161			return defaultValue
162		}
163	
164		// Direct type conversions for common numeric types
165		switch v := val.(type) {
166		case float64:
167			debugf("%s: %f", option, v)
168			return v
169		case int:
170			value := float64(v)
171			debugf("%s: %f", option, value)
172			return value
173		case int64:
174			value := float64(v)
175			debugf("%s: %f", option, value)
176			return value
177		case uint64:
178			value := float64(v)
179			debugf("%s: %f", option, value)
180			return value
181		default:
182			debugf("%s: %f", option, defaultValue)
183			return defaultValue
184		}
185	}
186	
187	func (m Map) Int(option Option, defaultValue int) int {
188		val, found := m[option]
189		if !found {
190			debugf("%s: %d", option, defaultValue)
191			return defaultValue
192		}
193	
194		// Direct type conversions for common numeric types
195		switch v := val.(type) {
196		case int:
197			debugf("%s: %d", option, v)
198			return v
199		case int64:
200			value := int(v)
201			debugf("%s: %d", option, value)
202			return value
203		case uint64:
204			value := int(v)
205			debugf("%s: %d", option, value)
206			return value
207		case float64:
208			value := int(v)
209			debugf("%s: %d", option, value)
210			return value
211		default:
212			debugf("%s: %d", option, defaultValue)
213			return defaultValue
214		}
215	}
216	
217	func (m Map) KeyValueMap(option Option, defaultValue map[string]string) map[string]string {
218		val, found := m[option]
219		if !found {
220			debugf("%s: %v", option, defaultValue)
221			return defaultValue
222		}
223	
224		keyValues := parseKeyValueArray(val)
225		debugf("%s: %v", option, keyValues)
226		return keyValues
227	}
228	
229	func (m Map) StringArray(option Option, defaultValue []string) []string {
230		val, found := m[option]
231		if !found {
232			debugf("%s: %v", option, defaultValue)
233			return defaultValue
234		}
235	
236		keyValues := ParseStringArray(val)
237		debugf("%s: %v", option, keyValues)
238		return keyValues
239	}
240	
241	func (m Map) Any(option Option, defaultValue any) any {
242		val, found := m[option]
243		if !found {
244			debugf("%s: %v", option, defaultValue)
245			return defaultValue
246		}
247	
248		debugf("%s: %v", option, val)
249		return val
250	}
251	
252	func ParseStringArray(param any) []string {
253		return generics.ParseStringSlice(param)
254	}
255	
256	func parseKeyValueArray(param any) map[string]string {
257		switch v := param.(type) {
258		default:
259			return map[string]string{}
260		case map[any]any:
261			keyValueArray := make(map[string]string)
262			for key, value := range v {
263				val := value.(string)
264				keyString := fmt.Sprintf("%v", key)
265				keyValueArray[keyString] = val
266			}
267			return keyValueArray
268		case map[string]any:
269			keyValueArray := make(map[string]string)
270			for key, value := range v {
271				val := value.(string)
272				keyValueArray[key] = val
273			}
274			return keyValueArray
275		case []any:
276			keyValueArray := make(map[string]string)
277			for _, s := range v {
278				l := ParseStringArray(s)
279				if len(l) == 2 {
280					key := l[0]
281					val := l[1]
282					keyValueArray[key] = val
283				}
284			}
285			return keyValueArray
286		case Map:
287			keyValueArray := make(map[string]string)
288			for key, value := range v {
289				val := value.(string)
290				keyString := fmt.Sprintf("%v", key)
291				keyValueArray[keyString] = val
292			}
293			return keyValueArray
294		case map[string]string:
295			return v
296		}
297	}
298	
299	// Generic functions
300	
301	type Value interface {
302		string | int | []string | float64 | bool
303	}
304	
305	func OneOf[T Value](options Provider, defaultValue T, props ...Option) T {
306		for _, prop := range props {
307			// get value on a generic get, then see if we can cast to T?
308			val := options.Any(prop, nil)
309			if val == nil {
310				continue
311			}
312	
313			if v, ok := val.(T); ok {
314				return v
315			}
316		}
317	
318		return defaultValue
319	}

input.description: Review language-segment migration plan
input.prompt:
You are reviewing an implementation plan for the oh-my-posh Go project (repo root D:\oh-my-posh, prompt-rendering CLI, Go module under src/). You have no prior context on this conversation — everything you need is below. Read the referenced files yourself before judging; don't take the plan's claims about the codebase on faith.

## Background

oh-my-posh has ~26 "language" segments (src/segments/*.go: fortran.go, clojure.go, rust.go, etc.), each a thin Go struct embedding a shared `Language` base (src/segments/language.go). A recent contributor PR (#7691) added a new `gcc.go` segment that is ~25 lines: it just declares file extensions, one executable name, one version-parsing regex, and calls the shared base's `Enabled()`. The maintainer (the user I'm working with) wants to stop merging one-off Go files for segments this simple, and instead expose a generic, config-driven segment for this whole class of "run an executable, regex a version out of it, show it when certain files are present" behavior.

We already agreed (in a prior analysis phase, not shown here) on the target design:
- A new struct `ConfiguredLanguage` (embeds `Language`) with a shared `Enabled()`.
- Its behavior is driven by a `languageDefinitions` map (`map[string]languageDefinition`) of built-in presets — one entry per currently-simple language, copied verbatim from the file being deleted (extensions, project files, folders, tooling map of {executable, args, regex, version_url_template}, defaultTooling order).
- A new public segment type, `"type": "language"`, registers `ConfiguredLanguage` with NO preset — driven entirely by new user-facing options: `name` (required, used as cache key and preset lookup key), `tools` (array of `{name, executable, args, regex, version_url_template}`, parsed from raw config since the options.Provider interface (src/segments/options/map.go) has no typed array-of-objects accessor — must use `options.Any()` + manual parsing, same pattern as `parseKeyValueArray` in that file), plus the *already-existing* `extensions`/`folders` options and a *new* `project_files` option (extensions/folders are already config-overridable per src/segments/language.go:150-151; project_files currently is not and needs the same treatment).
- The ~19 currently-simple segments (fortran, clojure, crystal, elixir, julia, kotlin, lua, ocaml, perl, php, r, ruby, rust, swift, v, vala, zig, dart — verified by reading each file) get migrated: their `.go`+`_test.go` files deleted, a `languageDefinitions` entry added instead, and their one-line registration in src/config/segment_types.go changed from `func() SegmentWriter { return &segments.X{} }` to something that constructs `ConfiguredLanguage` pre-loaded with that preset name. Their public `"type": "fortran"` etc. config strings and schema.json entries are UNCHANGED — this is meant to be invisible to end users, purely an internal consolidation.
- Explicitly staying bespoke (NOT migrated): java.go (dynamic JAVA_HOME executable path), haskell.go (stack_ghc_mode option drives branching + parent-file check), mojo.go (Pixi venv env-var detection), golang.go (parses go.mod/go.work instead of running a binary), python.go/node.go/dotnet.go (venv/package-manager/SDK-file logic, 150-225 lines each).
- Two known wrinkles already found and folded into the plan:
  1. `getName()` in src/segments/language.go:141-145 derives the version-cache key via `runtime.Caller(2)` inspecting the calling `.go` file's basename — this breaks for a shared struct with no per-language file, so `Enabled()` must be changed to only call `getName()` when `l.name` is still empty, and `ConfiguredLanguage` must set `l.name` explicitly (from the preset key, or from the new `name` option for the public type).
  2. Zig's `InProjectDir() bool` method (currently only on `zig.go`, returning `zig.projectRoot != nil`) is a *documented, user-facing template field* (`.InProjectDir`, see website/docs/segments/languages/zig.mdx line 62), not dead code as it first appeared (only zig_test.go referenced it in a code grep, which looked like it might be unused — checking docs turned up the real usage). Plan: move this method from Zig onto the shared `Language` base itself before deleting zig.go, so every language segment gets `.InProjectDir` for free and Zig's migration needs no special-casing.
  3. `ruby.go` has one line of post-`Enabled()` cleanup (`if r.Full == "______" { r.Full = "" }`) that isn't representable as pure data. Plan: add an optional `sanitizeVersion func(string) string` hook field to `languageDefinition`, nil for everyone except ruby.

## Proposed task split

1. **Task 1 (Sonnet, main tree, sequential/first):** Core engine. Edit language.go (getName guard, add `ProjectFiles`/`Tools`/`Name` options, move `InProjectDir()` up from Zig). New `configured_language.go`: `ConfiguredLanguage` struct, `Enabled()`, `parseTools()` helper, `languageDefinitions` map seeded with just 2 proof entries (`fortran` — trivial single-tool; `ruby` — multi-tool fallback + the sanitizeVersion hook) to validate the pattern on both a simple and an edge case. Delete fortran.go/fortran_test.go/ruby.go/ruby_test.go, add equivalent table-driven tests, update their 2 registration lines. Verify: `go build ./...`, `go test ./src/segments/... -run "Fortran|Ruby|ConfiguredLanguage"`, `golangci-lint run ./src/segments/...`.
2. **Task 2 (Sonnet, main tree, depends on Task 1):** Public `"type": "language"` segment. Register it in segment_types.go pointing at a name-less `ConfiguredLanguage`. Add schema.json block documenting `name`, `tools`, `extensions`, `project_files`, `folders`. New docs page website/docs/segments/languages/language.mdx with a worked example that reproduces the GCC PR's behavior purely via config, linked from website/sidebars.js. Verify: build, then a live functional check — run oh-my-posh with a sample `type: language` config against a repo containing a C file.
3. **Tasks 3–6 (Sonnet, main tree, SEQUENTIAL batches, not parallel worktrees):** Migrate the remaining 17 languages in 4 batches of 4–5 (grouped roughly alphabetically). Each batch: add table entries (fields copied byte-for-byte from the file being deleted — regexes must not be retyped/paraphrased), delete old `.go`+`_test.go`, port test assertions into table-driven tests, update registration lines. Explicitly planned as sequential-in-main-tree rather than parallel worktrees because every batch touches the same `languageDefinitions` map literal and the same block of segment_types.go, which plan.md's own guidance says to sequence rather than risk parallel-worktree merge conflicts on.
4. **Task 7 (orchestrator, never delegated):** Full verification — full test suite, lint, schema validation, spot-check a few live segments in a real shell, then conventional-commit (likely one commit per task/batch).

## What I want from you

Critically review this plan against the actual codebase (read src/segments/language.go, a sample of 3-4 of the "simple" language files being migrated, src/segments/options/map.go, and src/config/segment_types.go yourself — don't just trust my summary). Specifically:

1. Are there other wrinkles like the Zig `.InProjectDir` or Ruby `sanitizeVersion` cases hiding in the 19 files being migrated that this plan doesn't account for? (I read all 19 already, but a second pass focused specifically on "what's NOT pure config here" is exactly the kind of thing worth double-checking.)
2. Is the `getName()`/cache-key fix (guard + explicit `l.name`) actually sufficient, or does the `runtime.Caller(2)` trick get used anywhere else that would break?
3. Is sequencing the migration batches (vs. parallelizing in worktrees) the right call given the actual size of the diffs, or is the merge-conflict risk overstated for a mechanical, well-scoped change?
4. Any concern with silently swapping the underlying Go type behind existing `"type": "fortran"` etc. strings — e.g., anything in the codebase (tests, theme-export code, JSON schema `$ref`s, or a Go API consumer) that type-asserts on the concrete struct type (`*segments.Fortran`) rather than the `SegmentWriter` interface, which would break even though the public config surface doesn't change?
5. Is the overall task split reasonable for delegation to a Sonnet-tier implementer, or should any task be split further / merged?

Report back: a numbered list of concrete findings (each either "confirmed risk, here's the fix" or "checked, not an issue"), plus any additional wrinkle you find. Keep it under 500 words — this is a plan review, not a rewrite.

content.text:
Fable 5's review turned up two real breakages I'd missed, plus one accounting slip on my part — verified both against the code before folding them in:

**Confirmed and fixed:**
1. **Dart/Flutter share code.** [dart.go:4-5](src/segments/dart.go:4) defines `dartExtensions`/`dartFolders` package vars that [flutter.go:14-15](src/segments/flutter.go:14) (staying bespoke) also uses — deleting dart.go as planned would break the flutter.go build. Fix: those two vars move into flutter.go as part of the Dart migration step.
2. **Two registration points, not one.** [segment_types.go](src/config/segment_types.go:27) has a `gob.Register(&segments.X{})` line per segment *in addition to* the `SegmentWriters` map entry — it's how the on-disk segment cache round-trips concrete types. Each migration removes its own `gob.Register` line; Task 1 adds one `gob.Register(&segments.ConfiguredLanguage{})` to cover all of them. Stale cached blobs from the old types just miss once and re-render (`segment.go:444-448`) — no user-visible break.
3. **My language list dropped Nim** when I typed it into the review prompt — real count is 19, not 18.

**Confirmed non-issues:** `getName()`'s guard-plus-explicit-name fix is sufficient and `runtime.Caller(2)` isn't used elsewhere; no concrete-type assertions exist outside `segment_types.go` so the type swap is safe; Clojure's conditional `defaultTooling` init is provably equivalent to plain data; sequencing the migration batches (vs. parallel worktrees) is right — every batch touches the same three shared blocks (`languageDefinitions`, `gob.Register`, `SegmentWriters`).

**Two practical notes taken:** preset keys in `languageDefinitions` must match the *old file basenames* exactly (`"r"`, `"v"`, `"ocaml"`, not struct names) so device version-cache keys survive the migration untouched; and prefer a `NewConfiguredLanguage(name)` constructor over an exported `Name` field, so it doesn't leak into templates/gob as an accidental public property.

## Final plan

1. **Task 1 (Sonnet, main tree, first):** `language.go` — guard `getName()`, add `ProjectFiles`/`Tools` options, move `InProjectDir()` off Zig onto `Language` itself. New `configured_language.go` — `ConfiguredLanguage`, `NewConfiguredLanguage(name)`, `parseTools()`, `languageDefinitions` seeded with `fortran` + `ruby` (proof cases: trivial, and multi-tool + the `sanitizeVersion` hook for Ruby's `"______"` cleanup). Delete fortran/ruby `.go`+`_test.go`, port tests, update both registration lines for each. Verify: build, targeted tests, lint.
2. **Task 2 (Sonnet, depends on 1):** Public `"type": "language"` segment — no preset, driven by `name`/`tools`/`extensions`/`project_files`/`folders`. Schema entry, new docs page reproducing the GCC PR via config, sidebar link. Functional check with an executable actually present on this machine (`go`, not `gcc`), GCC config kept as the docs example only.
3. **Tasks 3–6 (Sonnet, sequential, main tree):** Remaining 17 in ~4 batches — clojure/crystal/elixir/julia/kotlin/lua, then nim/ocaml/perl/php/r, then ruby(done)/rust/swift/v/vala, then zig/dart (dart batch also relocates the shared vars into flutter.go). Regex/extension/URL fields copied byte-for-byte from the deleted file, not retyped.
4. **Task 7 (me, not delegated):** Full build/test/lint, schema validation, live spot-check of a few migrated segments in a real shell, conventional commits.

This is a plan only — nothing's been touched yet. Want me to move to Phase 3 and start delegating Task 1?

6	## Executor matrix
7	
8	Tier definitions and per-vendor model examples: [model-tiers.md](model-tiers.md).
9	
10	| Task profile                                             | Executor                                                       |
11	| ---------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------ |
12	| Trivial: mechanical edit, config tweak, typo, doc update  | Do it directly, or batch on a trivial-tier model              |
13	| Standard, well-specified implementation                   | Coordinator itself, or a same-tier subagent for parallelism   |
14	| Hits an escalation trigger (see [escalate.md](escalate.md)) | Escalation-tier subagent, for that one specific question    |
15	
16	- The coordinator typically runs at implementer tier itself, so standard tasks are usually
17	  executed directly rather than delegated. Delegate anyway when there's real parallelism —
18	  independent tasks, each in its own worktree — or to shed trivial mechanical work onto a
19	  cheaper model.
20	- Escalation is never the default path for "ambiguous" or "architectural" — it fires only on the
21	  concrete triggers in [escalate.md](escalate.md), and only for the specific question, not the
22	  whole task.
23	
24	## What a delegation carries
25	
26	Hand the subagent its full pinned spec from Phase 2, plus:
27	
28	- The verification commands it must run and pass before reporting done.
29	- The instruction to report what it changed and what it verified — not just "done".
30	- The instruction to stop and report when it hits something the spec does not cover, instead of
31	  improvising scope.
32	
33	## What is never delegated downward
34	
35	Analysis, final verification, and delivery never go to an implementer — they stay with the
36	coordinator or go up to Escalation tier for a specific question:
37	
38	- Analysis and root-cause work (Phase 1).
39	- Final verification (Phase 5) — an implementer's green run is a claim, not a result.
40	- Commits, history rewrites, pushes, and user-facing reporting (Phase 6) — these stay with the
41	  coordinator regardless of tier; they're about accountability, not capability, so they never go
42	  to Escalation tier either.
43

1	# Model tiers and agent tooling
2	
3	The workflow talks about capability tiers, not vendor names. Map whatever stack is in use onto
4	these four tiers. The model names below are a mid-2026 snapshot — they go stale; when a name no
5	longer exists, map its replacement by tier, not by nostalgia.
6	
7	## The four tiers
8	
9	| Tier        | Role                                       | Anthropic         | OpenAI            | Google            |
10	| ----------- | ------------------------------------------- | ----------------- | ----------------- | ----------------- |
11	| Escalation  | Judgment calls the coordinator flags        | Fable 5, Opus 4.8 | Sol, GPT-5 (high) | Gemini 2.5 Pro    |
12	| Coordinator | Resident; owns every phase by default       | Sonnet 5          | GPT-5, GPT-4.1    | Gemini 2.5 Flash  |
13	| Implementer | Pinned-spec work, often same tier as coordinator | Sonnet 5     | GPT-5, GPT-4.1    | Gemini 2.5 Flash  |
14	| Trivial     | Mechanical edits                            | Haiku 4.5         | GPT-4.1 mini      | Gemini Flash-Lite |
15	
16	- **Escalation** — the strongest reasoning model available, called only when a trigger in
17	  [escalate.md](escalate.md) fires: unclear root cause, architectural risk, security sensitivity,
18	  an irreversible operation, repeated spec gaps, or low confidence in a review. Answers one
19	  specific question, then hands control back to the coordinator.
20	- **Coordinator** — a capable mid-tier model, resident for the whole task. Owns analysis,
21	  planning, supervision, and verification by default. It's capable enough for the large majority
22	  of work, and cheap enough relative to Escalation tier that most tasks never need to call up at
23	  all — that's the point of the split.
24	- **Implementer** — often literally the same model as the coordinator; the distinction is
25	  parallelism and workspace isolation, not capability. Falls back to a smaller model for batches
26	  of mechanical, unambiguous edits.
27	- **Trivial** — small, very fast models for mechanical, unambiguous edits: renames, config
28	  tweaks, typo fixes, doc touch-ups. Batch several to amortize the dispatch overhead. When in
29	  doubt between trivial and implementer, pick implementer — a wrong cheap edit costs more than
30	  the price difference.
31	
32	## Tooling mappings
33	
34	How to run the coordinator/escalation/implementer split in common agent tooling:
35	
36	- **Claude Code** — run the session itself on a coordinator-tier model (e.g. Sonnet 5). Call
37	  `Agent` with `model: opus` (or `fable`) only at an escalation checkpoint, scoped to the one
38	  question that triggered it. Use worktree isolation for parallel implementer-tier tasks, and
39	  `model: haiku` for batched trivial edits.
40	- **GitHub Copilot** — run Copilot Chat on a coordinator-tier model for the whole flow; switch the
41	  model picker to the strongest reasoning model only for the specific question an escalation
42	  trigger flagged, then switch back. Standalone, well-specified tasks can still be assigned to the
43	  Copilot coding agent (assign the issue or PR to Copilot), which works on its own branch — the
44	  pinned spec becomes the issue body.
45	- **Codex** — orchestrate locally (CLI or chat) on a coordinator-tier model; dispatch each pinned
46	  spec as a Codex cloud task, one task per independent unit, and review the resulting diffs
47	  yourself, escalating to the frontier model only when a trigger fires.
48	- **Cursor and similar IDE agents** — one composer/agent session per task on a coordinator-tier
49	  model; background agents for the parallel implementer-tier ones; switch to the strongest model
50	  in-session only for an escalation checkpoint, then switch back.
51	
52	**No subagent support at all?** Keep the phases, drop the parallelism: run the whole flow on a
53	coordinator-tier model, and switch the session model up to the strongest one only for the specific
54	question an escalation trigger flagged, then switch back down. The discipline transfers even when
55	the delegation mechanism does not.
56

# Go Development Instructions

Follow idiomatic Go practices and community standards when writing Go code.
These instructions are based on [Effective Go](https://go.dev/doc/effective_go),
[Go Code Review Comments](https://go.dev/wiki/CodeReviewComments),
and [Google's Go Style Guide](https://google.github.io/styleguide/go/).

## General Instructions

- Write simple, clear, and idiomatic Go code
- Favor clarity and simplicity over cleverness
- Follow the principle of least surprise
- Keep the happy path left-aligned (reduce indentation)
- Return early to reduce nesting
- Make the zero value useful
- Document exported types, functions, methods, and packages
- Use Go modules for dependency management
- **AVOID `else` statements - use early returns, continue, or break instead**
- Avoid wrapping primitives without a clear semantic benefit; define new types when they add meaning.
- Use typed slices/maps and document element semantics when not obvious.
- Start error strings with a lowercase letter.

## Naming Conventions

### Packages

- Use lowercase, single-word package names
- Avoid `_` characters, hyphens, or mixedCaps
- Choose names that describe what the package provides, not what it contains
- Avoid generic names like `util`, `common`, or `base`
- Package names should be singular, not plural

### Variables and Functions

- Use mixedCaps or MixedCaps (camelCase) rather than `_` characters
- Keep names short but descriptive
- Use single-letter variables for very short scopes (like loop indices)
- Exported names start with a capital letter
- Unexported names start with a lowercase letter
- Avoid stuttering (e.g., avoid `http.HTTPServer`, prefer `http.Server`)

### Interfaces

- Name interfaces with -er suffix when possible (e.g., `Reader`, `Writer`, `Formatter`)
- Single-method interfaces should be named after the method (e.g., `Read` → `Reader`)
- Keep interfaces small and focused

### Constants

- Use MixedCaps for exported constants
- Use mixedCaps for unexported constants
- Group related constants using `const` blocks
- Consider using typed constants for better type safety

## Code Style and Formatting

### Formatting

- Always use `gofmt` to format code
- Use `goimports` to manage imports automatically
- Keep line to 180 max at all times
- Add blank lines to separate logical groups of code

### Comments

- Write comments in complete sentences
- Start sentences with the name of the thing being described
- Package comments should start with "Package [name]"
- Use line comments (`//`) for most comments
- Use block comments (`/* */`) sparingly, mainly for package documentation
- Document why, not what, unless the what is complex

### Error Handling

- Check errors immediately after the function call
- Don't ignore errors using `_` unless you have a good reason (document why)
- Wrap errors with context using `fmt.Errorf` with `%w` verb
- Create custom error types when you need to check for specific errors
- Place error returns as the last return value
- Name error variables `err`
- Keep error messages lowercase and don't end with punctuation

### Logging

- Always use the codebase `log` package for logging
- Log errors at the point they occur using `log.Error(err)`
- Do not format the errors, let the `log` package handle it
- For complex function calls, use `defer log.Trace(time.Now(), args)`
    where args are the function arguments at the start of the function.

### Control Flow

- **NEVER use `else` statements** - they create unnecessary nesting and reduce readability
- Use early returns to handle error cases and edge conditions first
- Use `continue` in loops to skip to the next iteration instead of nesting
- Use `break` to exit loops early instead of complex conditional logic
- Keep the main logic (happy path) left-aligned with minimal indentation

**❌ BAD - Don't do this:**

```go
func processEntry(entry *Entry) string {
    if entry.Expired() {
        return "expired"
    } else {
        if entry.TTL < 0 {
            return "never expires"
        } else {
            return fmt.Sprintf("expires at %s", time.Unix(entry.Timestamp, 0))
        }
    }
}
```

**✅ GOOD - Do this instead:**

```go
func processEntry(entry *Entry) string {
    if entry.Expired() {
        return "expired"
    }

    if entry.TTL < 0 {
        return "never expires"
    }

    return fmt.Sprintf("expires at %s", time.Unix(entry.Timestamp, 0))
}
```

**❌ BAD - Nested loop logic:**

```go
for _, item := range items {
    if item.IsValid() {
        if item.ShouldProcess() {
            // complex processing logic
        }
    }
}
```

**✅ GOOD - Early continue:**

```go
for _, item := range items {
    if !item.IsValid() {
        continue
    }
    if !item.ShouldProcess() {
        continue
    }

    // complex processing logic (happy path)
}
```

## Architecture and Project Structure

### Package Organization

- Follow standard Go project layout conventions
- Group related functionality into packages
- Avoid circular dependencies

### Dependency Management

- Use Go modules (`go.mod` and `go.sum`)
- Keep dependencies minimal
- Regularly update dependencies for security patches
- Use `go mod tidy` to clean up unused dependencies
- Vendor dependencies when necessary

## Type Safety and Language Features

### Type Definitions

- Define types to add meaning and type safety
- Use struct tags for JSON, YAML and TOML on exported fields
- Prefer explicit type conversions
- Use type assertions carefully and check the second return value

### Pointers vs Values

- Use pointers for large structs or when you need to modify the receiver
- Use values for small structs and when immutability is desired
- Be consistent within a type's method set
- Consider the zero value when choosing pointer vs value receivers

### Interfaces and Composition

- Accept interfaces, return concrete types
- Keep interfaces small (1-3 methods is ideal)
- Use embedding for composition
- Define interfaces close to where they're used, not where they're implemented
- Don't export interfaces unless necessary

## Concurrency

### Goroutines

- Don't create goroutines in libraries; let the caller control concurrency
- Always know how a goroutine will exit
- Use `sync.WaitGroup` or channels to wait for goroutines
- Avoid goroutine leaks by ensuring cleanup

### Channels

- Use channels to communicate between goroutines
- Don't communicate by sharing memory; share memory by communicating
- Close channels from the sender side, not the receiver
- Use buffered channels when you know the capacity
- Use `select` for non-blocking operations

### Synchronization

- Use `sync.Mutex` for protecting shared state
- Keep critical sections small
- Use `sync.RWMutex` when you have many readers
- Prefer channels over mutexes when possible
- Use `sync.Once` for one-time initialization

## Error Handling Patterns

### Creating Errors

- Use `errors.New` for simple static errors
- Use `fmt.Errorf` for errors with runtime values
- Create custom error types for domain-specific errors
- Export error variables for sentinel errors
- Use `errors.Is` and `errors.As` for error checking

### Error Propagation

- Add context when propagating errors up the stack
- Don't log and return errors (choose one)
- Handle errors at the appropriate level
- Consider using structured errors for better debugging

## Performance Optimization

### Memory Management

- Minimize allocations in hot paths
- Reuse objects when possible (consider `sync.Pool`)
- Use value receivers for small structs
- Preallocate slices when size is known
- Avoid unnecessary string conversions

### Profiling

- Use built-in profiling tools (`pprof`)
- Benchmark critical code paths
- Profile before making performance changes
- Focus on algorithmic improvements first
- Consider using `testing.B` for benchmarks

## Testing

### Test Organization

- Keep tests in the same package (white-box testing)
- Use `_test` package suffix for black-box testing
- Name test files with `_test.go` suffix
- Place test files next to the code they test

### Writing Tests

- Name tests descriptively using `TestFunctionNameScenario`
- Use subtests with `t.Run` for better organization
- Test both success and error cases
- Use `testify/assert` and `testify/require` for assertions
- Include both positive and negative test cases
- Test edge cases and error conditions
- When including a standard library that conflicts with an existing import,
  use the lib(library name) pattern to avoid conflicts.
  Such as: `libtime` for the `time` package.

#### Table-driven tests are the default

One behavior under test = one test function with a table of cases. Never write several
near-identical test functions that differ only in input data, fixtures, or expected outcome —
those differences are table fields. A per-case fixture (a different map, config, or mock return)
is not a reason to split; put the fixture in the table. Shared setup (mocks, caches, `Init`
calls) runs once before the loop.

When adding cases to an existing test file, extend the existing table instead of adding a new
test function.

Only split into separate test functions when the flow genuinely differs: a different API under
test, or a setup/assertion sequence that cannot be expressed as table fields.

```go
// ✅ CORRECT: fixture and error expectation are table fields
cases := []struct {
    Fixture       Palette
    Case          string
    Input         Ansi
    Expected      Ansi
    ExpectedError bool
}{
    {Case: "literal", Fixture: Palette{"a": "#123456"}, Input: "p:a", Expected: "#123456"},
    {Case: "invalid", Fixture: Palette{"a": "{{ broken"}, Input: "p:a", ExpectedError: true},
}

// ❌ WRONG: TestResolveLiteral, TestResolveReference, TestResolveInvalid —
// three functions repeating the same setup with different data
```

### Test Helpers

- Mark helper functions with `t.Helper()`
- Create test fixtures for complex setup
- Use `testing.TB` interface for functions used in tests and benchmarks
- Clean up resources using `t.Cleanup()`

#### Global state: always save the original value and restore it

When a test mutates package-level variables (resolvers, loggers, clocks, `time.Local`, etc.),
save the original value into a local variable and restore it via `t.Cleanup`. Never restore to a
hardcoded value; you would overwrite whatever state preceded your test.

```go
// ✅ CORRECT: save original, restore original
origResolver := myPackageResolver
t.Cleanup(func() { myPackageResolver = origResolver })
myPackageResolver = fakeResolver

origLocal := time.Local
t.Cleanup(func() { time.Local = origLocal })
time.Local = time.UTC

// ❌ WRONG: restores to a hardcoded value instead of the pre-test value
defer func() { time.Local = time.FixedZone("UTC", 0) }()
```

## Security Best Practices

### Input Validation

- Validate all external input
- Use strong typing to prevent invalid states
- Sanitize data before using in SQL queries
- Be careful with file paths from user input
- Validate and escape data for different contexts (HTML, SQL, shell)

### Cryptography

- Use standard library crypto packages
- Never write your own cryptography
- Use crypto/rand for random number generation
- Store passwords using bcrypt or similar
- Use TLS for network communication

## Documentation

### Code Documentation

- Document all exported symbols
- Start documentation with the symbol name
- Use examples in documentation when helpful
- Keep documentation close to code
- Update documentation when code changes

### README and Documentation Files

- Include clear setup instructions
- Document dependencies and requirements
- Provide usage examples
- Document configuration options
- Include troubleshooting section

## Tools and Development Workflow

### Essential Tools

- `go fmt`: Format code
- `go vet`: Find suspicious constructs
- `golint` or `golangci-lint`: Additional linting
- `go test`: Run tests
- `go mod`: Manage dependencies
- `go generate`: Code generation

### Development Practices

- Run tests before committing
- Use pre-commit hooks for formatting and linting
- Keep commits focused and atomic
- Write clear, descriptive commit messages
- Review diffs before committing

### Pre-Commit Quality Gate

**REQUIRED BEFORE EVERY COMMIT.** Run the following commands in sequence after any Go code
change. Commit after all pass with zero errors. Never skip this step; these
linters catch real bugs and style violations that will be flagged in CI or code review.

1. **Code Modernization**: Apply modern Go best practices; this rewrites files in place

   ```bash
   modernize --fix "./..."
   ```

   > `modernize` modifies source files (e.g. replacing `strings.Split`
   > with `strings.SplitSeq` for Go 1.24+ range loops). Always stage its changes and
   > include them in the same commit as your feature code.

2. **Field Alignment**: Optimize struct field ordering for memory efficiency; this rewrites files in place

   ```bash
   fieldalignment --fix "./..."
   ```

   > **Warning:** `fieldalignment` rewrites struct field order. Any inline struct
   > literals that use **positional** (unnamed) field initialization (common in
   > table-driven test files) will break after the reorder.
   > **Always use named fields** in struct literals (e.g. `{Case: "foo", Now: t}`)
   > so that the order of fields in the struct definition does not matter.

3. **Dependency Management**: Clean up and organize module dependencies

   ```bash
   go mod tidy
   ```

4. **Formatting and Linting**: Ensure code follows standards (**must report zero errors**)

   ```bash
   gofmt -w .
   golangci-lint run
   ```

After steps 1 through 3, always run `git diff` to review auto-applied changes before staging them.
All four steps must complete with zero errors before the commit is created.

#### Platform-specific files (`_unix.go`, `_windows.go`, `_darwin.go`)

If you add or modify a file with a platform-specific suffix, also cross-compile to catch
issues the local OS linter skips. On Windows, run:

```powershell
$env:GOOS = "linux"; go build ./...; $env:GOOS = ""
```

On Linux/macOS, run:

```bash
GOOS=windows go build ./...
```

This catches import mismatches, missing symbols, and linter rules (like `modernize`
`strings.SplitSeq`) that apply on the non-host platform.

#### Common golangci-lint violations to fix before committing

These rules frequently fire on new code and are quick to resolve before linting:

| Linter | Trigger | Fix |
| ------ | ------- | --- |
| `goconst` | Same string literal occurs 3+ times | Extract to a named `const` |
| `gofmt` | Incorrect indentation or comment spacing | Run `gofmt -w .`; it fixes automatically |
| `dupl` | Two functions/test cases with near-identical structure | Add `//nolint:dupl` with a brief reason comment |
| `modernize` | `strings.Split` used in a `for range` (Go 1.24+) | Run `modernize --fix "./..."` (auto-fixes) |

## Common Pitfalls to Avoid

- Not checking errors
- Ignoring race conditions
- Creating goroutine leaks
- Not using defer for cleanup
- Modifying maps concurrently
- Confusing nil interfaces with nil pointers
- Forgetting to close resources (files, connections)
- Using global variables unnecessarily
- Over-using empty interfaces (`interface{}` or `any`)
- Not considering the zero value of types

package segments

import (
	"fmt"
	"testing"

	"github.com/jandedobbeleer/oh-my-posh/src/segments/options"
	"github.com/jandedobbeleer/oh-my-posh/src/shell"

	"github.com/stretchr/testify/assert"
)

func TestConfiguredLanguageFortranPreset(t *testing.T) {
	cases := []struct {
		Case           string
		ExpectedString string
		Version        string
	}{
		{
			Case:           "GNU Fortran 10.2.1 Debian",
			ExpectedString: "10.2.1",
			Version: `GNU Fortran (Debian 10.2.1-6) 10.2.1 20210110
			Copyright (C) 2020 Free Software Foundation, Inc.
			This is free software; see the source for copying conditions.  There is NO
			warranty; not even for MERCHANTABILITY or FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE.`,
		},
		{
			Case:           "GNU Fortran 11.4.0 Ubuntu",
			ExpectedString: "11.4.0",
			Version: `GNU Fortran (Ubuntu 11.4.0-1ubuntu1~22.04) 11.4.0
			Copyright (C) 2021 Free Software Foundation, Inc.
			This is free software; see the source for copying conditions.  There is NO
			warranty; not even for MERCHANTABILITY or FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE.`,
		},
	}

	for _, tc := range cases {
		params := &mockedLanguageParams{
			cmd:           "gfortran",
			versionParam:  "--version",
			versionOutput: tc.Version,
			extension:     "*.f",
		}
		env, props := getMockedLanguageEnv(params)

		f := NewConfiguredLanguage("fortran")
		f.Init(props, env)

		assert.True(t, f.Enabled(), tc.Case)
		assert.Equal(t, tc.ExpectedString, renderTemplate(env, f.Template(), f), tc.Case)
	}
}

func TestConfiguredLanguageRubyPreset(t *testing.T) {
	cases := []struct {
		Case           string
		ExpectedString string
		Version        string
		HasRbenv       bool
		HasRvmprompt   bool
		HasChruby      bool
		HasAsdf        bool
		HasRuby        bool
		HasRubyFiles   bool
		HasRakeFile    bool
		HasGemFile     bool
		FetchVersion   bool
	}{
		{Case: "Ruby files", ExpectedString: "", FetchVersion: false, HasRubyFiles: true},
		{Case: "Rakefile", ExpectedString: "", FetchVersion: false, HasRakeFile: true},
		{Case: "Gemfile", ExpectedString: "", FetchVersion: false, HasGemFile: true},
		{Case: "Gemfile with version", ExpectedString: "err parsing info from ruby with", FetchVersion: true, HasGemFile: true},
		{
			Case:           "Version with chruby",
			ExpectedString: "ruby-2.6.3",
			FetchVersion:   true,
			HasRubyFiles:   true,
			HasChruby:      true,
			Version: ` * ruby-2.6.3
			ruby-1.9.3-p392
			jruby-1.7.0
			rubinius-2.0.0-rc1`,
		},
		{
			Case:           "Version with chruby line 2",
			ExpectedString: "ruby-1.9.3-p392",
			FetchVersion:   true,
			HasRubyFiles:   true,
			HasChruby:      true,
			Version: ` ruby-2.6.3
			* ruby-1.9.3-p392
			jruby-1.7.0
			rubinius-2.0.0-rc1`,
		},
		{
			Case:           "Version with asdf",
			ExpectedString: "2.6.3",
			FetchVersion:   true,
			HasRubyFiles:   true,
			HasAsdf:        true,
			Version:        "ruby            2.6.3           /Users/jan/Projects/oh-my-posh/.tool-versions",
		},
		{
			Case:           "Version with asdf not set",
			ExpectedString: "",
			FetchVersion:   true,
			HasRubyFiles:   true,
			HasAsdf:        true,
			Version:        "ruby            ______          No version set. Run \"asdf <global|shell|local> ruby <version>\"",
		},
		{
			Case:           "Version with ruby",
			ExpectedString: "2.6.3",
			FetchVersion:   true,
			HasRubyFiles:   true,
			HasRuby:        true,
			Version:        "ruby  2.6.3 (2019-04-16 revision 67580) [universal.x86_64-darwin20]",
		},
	}

	for _, tc := range cases {
		params := &mockedLanguageParams{
			cmd:           "ruby",
			versionParam:  "--version",
			versionOutput: tc.Version,
			extension:     "*.rb",
		}
		env, props := getMockedLanguageEnv(params)

		env.On("HasCommand", "rbenv").Return(tc.HasRbenv)
		env.On("RunCommandWithEnv", "rbenv", []string(nil), []string{"version-name"}).Return(tc.Version, nil)
		env.On("HasCommand", "rvm-prompt").Return(tc.HasRvmprompt)
		env.On("RunCommandWithEnv", "rvm-prompt", []string(nil), []string{"i", "v", "g"}).Return(tc.Version, nil)
		env.On("HasCommand", "chruby").Return(tc.HasChruby)
		env.On("RunCommandWithEnv", "chruby", []string(nil), []string(nil)).Return(tc.Version, nil)
		env.On("HasCommand", "asdf").Return(tc.HasAsdf)
		env.On("RunCommandWithEnv", "asdf", []string(nil), []string{"current", "ruby"}).Return(tc.Version, nil)
		env.On("HasFiles", "Rakefile").Return(tc.HasRakeFile)
		env.On("HasFiles", "Gemfile").Return(tc.HasGemFile)

		props[options.FetchVersion] = tc.FetchVersion

		ruby := NewConfiguredLanguage("ruby")
		ruby.Init(props, env)

		assert.True(t, ruby.Enabled(), tc.Case)
		assert.Equal(t, tc.ExpectedString, renderTemplate(env, ruby.Template(), ruby), tc.Case)
	}
}

// TestConfiguredLanguageCustomTools exercises the `tools` option path used by the public
// "language" segment type, which has no built-in preset and relies entirely on user config.
func TestConfiguredLanguageCustomTools(t *testing.T) {
	env := new(mock.Environment)
	env.On("HasCommand", "mytool").Return(true)
	env.On("RunCommandWithEnv", "mytool", []string(nil), []string{"--version"}).Return("mytool version 1.2.3", nil)
	env.On("HasFiles", "*.myl").Return(true)
	env.On("Pwd").Return("/usr/home/project")
	env.On("Home").Return("/usr/home")

	props := options.Map{
		options.FetchVersion: true,
		LanguageName:         "mylang",
		LanguageExtensions:   []string{"*.myl"},
		Tools: []any{
			map[string]any{
				"name":       "mytool",
				"executable": "mytool",
				"args":       []any{"--version"},
				"regex":      `mytool version (?P<version>(?P<major>[0-9]+).(?P<minor>[0-9]+).(?P<patch>[0-9]+))`,
			},
		},
	}

	l := &ConfiguredLanguage{}
	l.Init(props, env)

	assert.True(t, l.Enabled())
	assert.Equal(t, "1.2.3", renderTemplate(env, l.Template(), l))
	assert.Equal(t, []string{"mytool"}, l.defaultTooling)
}

1	# Phase 5 — Verify
2	
3	Verification is never delegated downward and runs on the final, merged state of the change. It
4	has two halves: the project's quality gates, and functional proof. Both are the coordinator's own
5	work by default.
6	
7	## Quality gates
8	
9	- Build, full test suite, formatters, and linters — all must pass with zero errors.
10	- Apply the project's language skill when one exists (for example the Go skill's pre-commit
11	  gate: `modernize`, `fieldalignment`, `go mod tidy`, `gofmt`, `golangci-lint`).
12	- Cross-compile when platform-specific files changed (`_windows.go`, `_unix.go`, and the like) —
13	  the local OS linter skips the other platform's rules.
14	
15	## Functional proof
16	
17	Tests passing is necessary, not sufficient. Run the real flow and confirm concrete outputs:
18	render the prompt, execute the command, hit the endpoint. Record the actual values observed —
19	the final report quotes them as evidence, not adjectives.
20	
21	When the user said they will do the manual validation, state exactly what they should check and
22	what the expected result is.
23	
24	## Escalate on high-stakes results
25	
26	Running the gates and the functional proof stays with the coordinator. If the result is
27	ambiguous, or the change is high-blast-radius (migrations, security, irreversible operations), get
28	the strongest available model to judge the evidence before declaring done — see
29	[references/escalate.md](references/escalate.md).
30	
31	## Documentation
32	
33	Documentation changes ship in the same change as the code they describe. Check for every
34	user-visible behavior change:
35	
36	- Project docs / website pages for the touched feature.
37	- README or setup instructions when flags, commands, or defaults changed.
38	
39	## On failure
40	
41	A failed gate or a wrong functional result sends the task back to Phase 4 (fix via the
42	implementer) or Phase 1 (the analysis was wrong). Never weaken a gate, skip a linter, or delete
43	a test to get to green.

input.prompt:
Repo: D:\oh-my-posh (Go module, working dir for Go commands is D:\oh-my-posh\src). You have no context from prior conversation — everything you need is below. This is a mechanical, pattern-following task: a previous change already built and proved out the pattern you must replicate exactly. Do not improvise beyond it.

## Background

oh-my-posh has many "language" segments (src/segments/*.go), each a small Go struct embedding a shared `Language` base (src/segments/language.go) that runs an executable, regexes a version out of it, and decides visibility from file extensions/project files/folders. A new shared engine, `ConfiguredLanguage` (src/segments/configured_language.go), replaces the old one-struct-per-language pattern with a single struct driven by a data table (`languageDefinitions`). Two languages (fortran, ruby) are already migrated and serve as your exact template — READ src/segments/configured_language.go and src/segments/configured_language_test.go FIRST and study their shape before touching anything.

## Your task: migrate 6 more languages the same way

Languages: **clojure, crystal, elixir, julia, kotlin, lua**

Source files to read and then delete: src/segments/clojure.go + clojure_test.go, crystal.go + crystal_test.go, elixir.go + elixir_test.go, julia.go + julia_test.go, kotlin.go + kotlin_test.go, lua.go + lua_test.go.

For EACH of the 6 languages, do the following:

### 1. Add an entry to `languageDefinitions` in configured_language.go

Read the language's `.go` file and copy its `extensions`, `projectFiles`, `folders`, `tooling` map (`executable`, `args`, `regex`, and per-tool `versionURLTemplate` if present), `defaultTooling`, and language-level `versionURLTemplate` (if present) EXACTLGY as written — do not paraphrase or retype regexes from memory, copy them character-for-character from the source file. The map key must be the lowercase language name matching its existing `SegmentType` string in src/config/segment_types.go (e.g. `"clojure"`, `"crystal"`, `"elixir"`, `"julia"`, `"kotlin"`, `"lua"`).

209	// registered under name. Used by segment_types.go to back the migrated segment types.
210	func NewConfiguredLanguage(name string) *ConfiguredLanguage {
211		c := &ConfiguredLanguage{}
212		c.name = name
213		return c
214	}

216	func (c *ConfiguredLanguage) Enabled() bool {
217		if c.name == "" {
218			c.name = c.options.String(LanguageName, "")
219		}
220	
221		if preset, ok := languageDefinitions[c.name]; ok {

228		}

249		}
250	
251		if c.tooling == nil {
252			c.tooling = make(map[string]*cmd, len(raw))
253		}
254

256	
257		for _, item := range raw {
258			entry, ok := toStringMap(item)
259			if !ok {
260				continue
261			}
262	
263			name := stringField(entry, "name")
264			if name == "" {
265				continue
266			}
267	
268			c.tooling[name] = &cmd{
269				executable:         stringField(entry, "executable"),
270				args:               options.ParseStringArray(entry["args"]),
271				regex:              stringField(entry, "regex"),
272				versionURLTemplate: stringField(entry, "version_url_template"),
273			}
274			order = append(order, name)
275		}
276	
277		if _, hasPreset := languageDefinitions[c.name]; !hasPreset {
278			c.defaultTooling = order
279		}
280	}

282	func toStringMap(value any) (map[string]any, bool) {
283		switch v := value.(type) {
284		case map[string]any:
285			return v, true
286		case map[any]any:
287			out := make(map[string]any, len(v))
288			for key, val := range v {
289				out[fmt.Sprint(key)] = val
290			}
291			return out, true
292		default:
293			return nil, false
294		}
295	}
296	
297	func stringField(entry map[string]any, key string) string {
298		value, ok := entry[key]
299		if !ok {
300			return ""
301		}
302		return fmt.Sprint(value)

zig.go currently has its own `InProjectDir() bool` method that returns `zig.projectRoot != nil`. This is a DOCUMENTED, user-facing template field (`.InProjectDir`, see website/docs/segments/languages/zig.mdx) — it is NOT dead code, do not drop the behavior. However, an earlier change already moved an identical method onto the shared `Language` base struct itself (check src/segments/language.go — search for `InProjectDir`, you'll find it already there, returning `l.projectRoot != nil`). Since `ConfiguredLanguage` embeds `Language`, it already has this method for free via Go method promotion. **Do not add an `InProjectDir()` method anywhere else** — just delete zig.go entirely (including its copy of the method) and confirm `.InProjectDir` still works through the already-existing base method. zig.go also has `projectFiles: []string{"build.zig"}` — that DOES need to go into your new `zig` entry in `languageDefinitions` (the `projectFiles` field), since that's what makes `.InProjectDir` become true.

### Wrinkle 2 — Dart and Flutter share code; Flutter is NOT being migrated and must keep compiling

dart.go currently declares two package-level vars used by BOTH dart.go and flutter.go:
```go

	dartFolders    = []string{".dart_tool"}
)

If you just delete dart.go, flutter.go will fail to compile (undefined `dartExtensions`/`dartFolders`). Before deleting dart.go, edit flutter.go to inline the literal values directly instead of referencing the vars:
```go
f.extensions = []string{"*.dart", "pubspec.yaml", "pubspec.yml", "pubspec.lock"}
f.folders = []string{".dart_tool"}
```
Then delete dart.go (and dart_test.go) as normal, and use those same literal extension/folder lists in your new `dart` entry in `languageDefinitions`.

Base directory for this skill: D:\oh-my-posh\.claude\skills\markdown

# Markdown Content Rules

The following markdown content rules are enforced in the validators:

1. Headings: Use appropriate heading levels (H2, H3, etc.) to structure your content.
  Do not use an H1 heading, as this will be generated based on the title.
2. Lists: Use bullet points or numbered lists for lists. Ensure proper
  indentation and spacing.
3. Code Blocks: Use fenced code blocks for code snippets. Specify the language
  for syntax highlighting.
4. Links: Use proper markdown syntax for links. Ensure that links are valid and
  accessible.
5. Images: Use proper markdown syntax for images. Include alt text for
  accessibility.
6. Tables: Use markdown tables for tabular data. Ensure proper formatting and
  alignment.
7. Line Length: Limit line length to 120 characters for readability.
8. Whitespace: Use appropriate whitespace to separate sections and improve readability.
9. Front Matter: Include YAML front matter at the beginning of the file with required metadata fields.

## Formatting and Structure

Follow these guidelines for formatting and structuring your markdown content:

- Headings: Use `##` for H2 and `###` for H3. Ensure that headings are used in a
  hierarchical manner. Recommend restructuring if content includes H4, and more
  strongly recommend for H5.
- Lists: Use `-` for bullet points and `1.` for numbered lists. Indent nested lists with two spaces.
- Code Blocks: Use fenced code blocks with a language for syntax highlighting:

  ```go
  fmt.Println("hello")
  ```

- Links:
  - Inline: `[Docs](https://example.com/docs)`
  - Reference-style: `[Docs][docs]` and add at the end of the page:
    `[docs]: https://example.com/docs`
- Images: Use `![alt text](image URL)` for images. Include a brief description
  of the image in the alt text.
- Tables: Use `|` to create tables. Ensure that columns are properly aligned
  and headers are included.
- Line Length: Break lines at 120 characters to improve readability. Use soft
  line breaks for long paragraphs.
- Whitespace: Use blank lines to separate sections and improve readability. Avoid excessive whitespace.

## Post-Edit Verification

After editing any `.md` or `.mdx` file, run the following command and resolve all reported
errors before considering the task complete:

```shell
npx markdownlint-cli2 <edited-file>
```

This closes the feedback loop so violations are caught and fixed immediately rather than
relying on an external CI run.

## Validation Requirements

Ensure compliance with the following validation requirements:

- Content Rules: Ensure that the content follows the markdown content rules
  specified above.
- Formatting: Ensure that the content is properly formatted and structured
  according to the guidelines.
- Validation: Run the validation tools to check for compliance with the rules
  and guidelines.

Required | | `tools` | `array` | `[]` | the executables that can report the version, tried in order until one succeeds. See [Tools](#tools) below. Required | | `home_enabled` | `boolean` | `false` | display the segment in the HOME folder or not | | `fetch_version` | `boolean` | `true` | fetch the version | | `cache_duration` | `string` | `none` | the duration for which the version will be cached. content.text:
Base directory for this skill: D:\oh-my-posh\.claude\skills\conventional-commit

# Conventional Commit

## Commit Message Structure

```text
<type>(<scope>): <description>

[optional body]

[optional footer(s)]
```

### Types

| Type       | When to use                                            |
| ---------- | ------------------------------------------------------ |
| `feat`     | A new feature                                          |
| `fix`      | A bug fix                                              |
| `docs`     | Documentation changes, no code                         |
| `style`    | Formatting, missing semicolons, etc. (no logic change) |
| `refactor` | Code change that is neither a fix nor a feature        |
| `perf`     | Performance improvement                                |
| `test`     | Adding or correcting tests                             |
| `ci`       | CI configuration changes                               |
| `chore`    | Maintenance tasks (updating deps, tooling, etc.)       |
| `revert`   | Reverts a previous commit                              |

Append `!` after the type/scope to signal a **breaking change**: `feat!:` or `feat(api)!:`
When a change breaks existing behavior, **both markers are mandatory**: the `!` suffix on the type
**and** the `BREAKING CHANGE:` footer. They always appear together — never one without the other.

### Scope

Optional. Use the name of the area affected, e.g., `segment`, `cache`, `config`, `ui`.
Omit when the change is truly cross-cutting.

### Description

- Required. One short imperative sentence, no period at the end. The full header line (type +
  scope + description) must be **72 characters or fewer**. Aim for **50 characters or fewer for
  the description itself** — this almost always keeps the full header within budget regardless
  of type and scope length.
- Use the imperative mood: "add", not "added" or "adds". Never past tense or present-third-person:
  ✗ `added`, `fixed`, `bumped`, `implemented` → ✓ `add`, `fix`, `bump`, `implement`.
- **Never mirror the input's phrasing.** If the request uses past-tense words (`updated`, `added`,
  `bumped`, `was removed`, `got regenerated`), convert them to imperative before writing the
  description: `update`, `add`, `bump`, `remove`, `regenerate`.

### Body

Optional. Add context about _why_ the change was made, not _what_. The diff shows that.
Wrap at 72 characters.

### Footer

Use for:

- `BREAKING CHANGE: <description>` (required when `!` is used; explains the break).
- Issue references: `Closes #123`, `Fixes #456`.
- Co-authors: `Co-Authored-By: Name <email>`.

## Workflow

1. Run `git status` to review changed files.
2. Run `git diff` and `git diff --cached` to inspect staged and unstaged changes.
3. Identify the **type** from the table above. Ask yourself: does this change **remove, rename, or
   alter existing behavior** that callers depend on? If yes → it is a breaking change: use `!`
   after the type/scope **and** add a `BREAKING CHANGE:` footer. Both markers are always required
   together.
4. Identify the **scope** from the files/area changed.
5. Write a short **description** in the imperative mood.
6. Add a **body** if the _why_ needs explanation.
7. Add a **footer** for breaking changes or issue references.
8. Stage the relevant files explicitly (avoid `git add -A`).
9. Commit with a message that preserves multi-line formatting when body/footer are present.

## Examples

```text
feat(segment): add Ramadan segment with Aladhan API
fix(cache): always store mod time
docs(readme): update installation instructions
refactor(config): simplify option parsing logic
chore(deps): bump github.com/shirou/gopsutil/v4
feat(segment)!: rename template property StartTime to Start

BREAKING CHANGE: template strings using .StartTime must be updated to .Start
```

## Validation Checklist

- [ ] Type is one of the allowed values in .commitlintrc.yml
- [ ] The commit message respects the rules defined in .commitlintrc.yml
- [ ] Scope (if present) reflects the actual area changed
- [ ] Description is imperative mood, no trailing period
- [ ] Full header line (type + scope + description) is 72 characters or fewer
- [ ] Both `!` after type/scope **and** `BREAKING CHANGE:` footer are present whenever the change breaks existing behavior
- [ ] No sensitive files staged (.env, credentials, etc.)

git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
refactor(segments): migrate 19 languages to a generic engine

Most language segments were ~20-30 line Go files that only declared
file extensions, an executable name, and a version regex before
delegating to the shared Language base. Every new simple language
(e.g. the recent GCC PR) meant another one-off struct, test file,
and registration to review and maintain.

Replace fortran, ruby, clojure, crystal, elixir, julia, kotlin, lua,
nim, ocaml, perl, php, r, rust, swift, v, vala, zig, and dart with a
single ConfiguredLanguage engine driven by a built-in preset table.
Public config surface (segment type strings, all existing options)
is unchanged; this is purely an internal consolidation.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"
